title: Drone Circle
intro:
There is a slowness to patching modular synthesizers.

I have a cable in hand and a signal path in mind. I plug the cable into the desired output. I listen carefully as each new connection changes the sound.

There is a process of creating music through a series of small decisions: turning knobs, adjusting sliders, plugging cables, and pressing buttons. I've always loved this slowness.
body_1: The slowness exists between what you can imagine and what you can make. The idea of reducing this distance may seem especially topical at the moment, but it has long been the core pursuit of creative technology.
body_2: 1950s Cologne, Karlheinz Stockhausen drew shapes on paper to score Studie II. one of the earliest pieces of electronic music. Drawing rectangles for notation and triangles for amplitude, these sketches predate both the MIDI roll and the Envelope.
body_3: 1960s England, Daphne Oram invented Oramics, a machine that reads ink sketches on film strips to describe specific sound parameters: pitch, timbre, volume, vibrato, duration. The photocells then read the drawings and outputs the sound in real time.
body_4: In a modular synth session, knobs, sliders, cables, buttons all need to be physically operated. Of course, this is preceded by some kind of intention. This intention can be specific or vague, with high or low predictability in mind. The unclear intentions and incorrect assumptions leave room for happy accidents—some of the best moments.
body_5: At 56, our commercial work begins with setting predictable goals and clarifying intentions. However, due to the nature of business, the creative process is often shrouded in chaos. Lately we’ve been building agentic workflows to make this chaos more accessible. With this framing, we turned to modular synths and wondered what it would be like if we replaced the physical operations with natural language.
body_6: Drone Circle is a sound engine and sonic performance. A host machine runs the Rust-based sound engine—with a graph of oscillators, filters, LFOs, and other modular primitives—and exposes it as a server over Ethernet. Participants plug into the network with their own computer and tools, their own agent. Describe the sound that you want: fifteen oscillators across a frequency range, into a quad filter, an LFO sweeping the resonance, and the engine builds it. You can compile your own WebAssembly plugins and attach them. You can build your own panels and they talk back to host over WebSockets. You can ask what an LFO is, see it, and hear it.
interactive_intro: This browser section mirrors the drone web panel structure: module cards, patch ports, animated cable routing, and a layer mixer bus. We ported the core primitives to a Web Audio graph so you can issue agent-like prompts on the left and inspect or modify the patch on the right.
closing_1: To learn more about Drone Circle or to try it out in person, we are hosting private demos at our office in Liberty Village this spring and summer.
